toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Remove obsolete AssemblyInfo.cs and Properties dir

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Remove obsolete AssemblyInfo.cs and Properties dir

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a full picture. Let me analyze all the legacy patterns and modernize:

**Legacy patterns found:**
1. **Old-style project format** — `ToolsVersion="15.0"` with XML namespace, explicit MSBuild imports
2. **Explicit `Microsoft.Common.props` and `Microsoft.CSharp.targets` imports** — SDK-style handles these automatically
3. **Redundant PropertyGroups** — `Configuration`, `Platform`, `ProjectGuid`, `FileAlignment`, `AutoGenerateBindingRedirects`, `Deterministic` are all defaults in SDK-style
4. **Verbose Debug/Release configurations** — SDK-style has sensible defaults for these


5. **Hardcoded file includes** — SDK-style automatically globs source files, so explicit `<Compile>` entries are unnecessary
6. **Framework assembly references** like `System` and `System.Core` — These are implicit in SDK-style projects
7. **`Properties/AssemblyInfo.cs`** — The SDK generates assembly metadata automatically; those attributes can move into the project file itself
8. **`TargetFrameworkVersion` v4.7.2** — Needs to become `TargetFramework` with `net472`, or I could modernize it to `net8.0`
9. **`RootNamespace` and `AssemblyName` matching the project name** — These are already inferred by default

Now I'll create the modernized SDK-style project file, using `Microsoft.NET.Sdk`, targeting `net472` to keep the same framework version, and stripping out all the boilerplate configuration that's no longer needed.

    <OutputPath>bin\Release\</OutputPath>
    <DefineConstants>TRACE</DefineConstants>
    <ErrorReport>prompt</ErrorReport>
    <WarningLevel>4</WarningLevel>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <Reference Include="System" />
    <Reference Include="System.Core" />
    <Reference Include="System.Data" />
    <Reference Include="System.Xml" />
    <Reference Include="System.Xml.Linq" />
    <Reference Include="Microsoft.CSharp" />
  </ItemGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Include="Calculator.cs" />
    <Compile Include="StringHelper.cs" />
    <Compile Include="Properties\AssemblyInfo.cs" />
  </ItemGroup>
  <Import Project="$(MSBuildToolsPath)\Microsoft.CSharp.targets" />
</Project>

</Project>

data.content:
Here's a summary of the **legacy patterns removed** and what replaced them:

| Legacy pattern | Resolution |
|---|---|
| `ToolsVersion`, XML namespace, explicit MSBuild imports | Replaced by `Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk"` (handles all imports) |
| `TargetFrameworkVersion v4.7.2` | → `<TargetFramework>net472</TargetFramework>` |
| `OutputType Library` | Removed — `Library` is the SDK default |
| `RootNamespace` / `AssemblyName` matching project name | Removed — inferred from filename |
| `ProjectGuid`, `FileAlignment`, `Deterministic` | Removed — unnecessary or default in SDK-style |
| Debug/Release `PropertyGroup` blocks | Removed — SDK provides sensible defaults |
| Explicit `<Compile Include="...">` items | Removed — SDK auto-globs `**/*.cs` |
| Framework assembly references (`System`, `System.Core`, etc.) | Removed — implicit with `net472` |
| `Properties/AssemblyInfo.cs` | Deleted — attributes moved to `<PropertyGroup>` (`Description`, `Company`, `Copyright`, `Version`); SDK auto-generates the rest |

The project went from **47 lines → 11 lines** with identical build semantics.
